epics.title: Workflow Discipline & Issue Lifecycle Improvements
epics.description: Improvements to enforce proper issue workflow and manage issue/epic lifecycle. Includes soft-blocking file writes without an active issue, and a deferred status to park items without closing them.
epics.body:
## Overview
Two complementary improvements to make the issue lifecycle more robust:

1. **Soft-block file writes** — enforce that the agent only modifies files when an issue is actively in-progress, via layered policy injection and tool-result warnings.
2. **Deferred status** — allow issues and epics to be parked without closing them, keeping the active view clean while preserving context.

## Implementation Plan

### Soft-block file writes (thlp7pym)
- `before_agent_start`: inject full workflow policy into system prompt explaining the issue lifecycle and the rule that file writes require an in-progress issue.
- `turn_start`: inject a lightweight status line each turn (`📍 Current: [issue:id] Title (in-progress)` or `⚠️ No issue in-progress`).
- `tool_result` hook on `write`, `edit`, and `bash` (matching write patterns: `>`, `>>`, `tee`, `sed -i`, `mv`, `cp`): append a soft warning when no issue is in-progress.
- **File:** `index.ts`

### Deferred status (fqdkjlyp)
- Add `deferred` as a valid status for issues and epics (side-channel, not part of the sequential flow).
- New tools: `issue_defer` / `epic_defer` (with optional reason), `issue_resume` / `epic_resume` (restores previous status).
- `issue_list` / `epic_list`: exclude deferred by default, add `include_deferred` flag.
- `next_work`: ignore deferred items entirely.
- **Files:** `types.ts`, `issues.ts`, `epics.ts`, `store.ts`, `tool-docs.ts`
successCriteria: Before-agent-start hook injects a clear workflow policy explaining that file writes require an in-progress issue
Each turn start injects a single-line status showing the current in-progress issue or a warning that none is active
Write/edit/bash tool results append a visible soft warning when no issue is in-progress and a file mutation pattern is detected

A closeReason field (done/deferred/wont-fix) exists on issues and epics, set via existing issue_close/epic_close tools — replaces the originally planned issue_defer/epic_defer tools (rejected: unnecessary complexity)
Deferring an epic auto-defers all linked open issues; reopening restores them — replaces the originally planned issue_resume/epic_resume tools (rejected: issue_reopen/epic_reopen already handle this)
issue_list and epic_list exclude deferred items by default, and include them when include_deferred: true is passed
next_work never surfaces deferred items
All changes are documented in tool-docs.ts and behave consistently with existing tools

research.content: The soft-block approach is intentionally a **warning**, not a hard block — the agent can still proceed but is reminded of the policy. This avoids workflow friction while still enforcing discipline through visibility. The `tool_result` hook is the most effective layer since it fires at the moment of the violation.

epics.closeMessage: All workflow discipline improvements delivered. Soft-block write gate, per-turn status injection, and closeReason (deferred/wont-fix) implemented. Original defer/resume tools rejected in favour of simpler closeReason on existing close tools.

epics.title: Core Architecture — Context Engine, Hook Registry & Debug
epics.description: Foundation layer: centralize all context injection, tool hooks, and message presentation into declarative registries with mode system, config toggles, and debug introspection.
epics.body:
This epic establishes the architectural foundation that all other features build on. Currently, context steering, tool hooks, and message formatting are scattered inline across `index.ts` with ad-hoc conditions. This epic centralizes them into three systems:

1. **Context Engine** — Declarative rules for all agent steering/injection with mode system and conditional composition
2. **Hook Registry** — Declarative tool lifecycle hooks with conditions, priorities, and mode filters
3. **Message Templates** — Separation of data vs. presentation for all tool outputs
4. **Debug Mode** — ENV-gated introspection tools to see exactly what the agent receives

### Build Order
1. Context Engine (establishes mode system, config patterns, debug infra)
2. Hook Registry (reuses mode system from Context Engine)
3. Debug Mode (introspects both systems)

### Why First
Every planned feature (onboarding, source alerts, folder guard, etc.) would otherwise add more scattered inline code. Building this first means new features are simple rule registrations.

epics.title: Data & Security — Attachments, Folder Guard & Source Alerts
epics.description: Add file attachments with Git LFS, protect the `.pi/project/` folder from direct agent access, and auto-notify when edited files are linked as asset sources.
epics.body:
This epic adds data management and security features:

1. **File Attachments** — Copy reference files into tracked `.pi/project/attachments/` with Git LFS for binaries. Applies to issues, epics, and assets.
2. **Folder Guard** — Block direct agent access to `.pi/project/` via bash/read/write/edit tool hooks. Scrub path references from context.
3. **Source File Alerts** — When a file linked as an asset source is edited, notify the agent to review the asset.

### Dependencies
- Hook Registry (folder guard and source alerts register as hooks)
- Context Engine (folder path scrubbing in context rules)

### Build Order
1. Folder Guard (security, uses Hook Registry)
2. Source File Alerts (uses Hook Registry)
3. File Attachments (independent, larger scope)

epics.title: Issue Traceability & File Capture
epics.description: Adds automatic file-change tracking to in-progress issues, giving each issue a living record of what was touched. Optionally gates closure behind per-file change notes for full traceability.
epics.body:
## Goal
Automatically track which files are created/written/edited during an in-progress issue and surface them as references on that issue. Optionally require a one-sentence change note per file before the issue can be closed.

## Approach
1. Two new config flags (`issues.capture_edited_files`, `issues.require_file_change_notes`), both default `false`
2. Hook pi write/edit tool events in `index.ts` — when capture is enabled and an issue is in-progress, add a `reference` research note with a distinguishable `[auto]` prefix and the file path (deduplicated)
3. Close gate in issue close logic — when `require_file_change_notes` is enabled, scan auto-captured references and block if any lack a follow-up change note

## Files Affected
- `config.ts` — new config entries
- `index.ts` — tool event hooks for write/edit
- `issues.ts` — close gate logic
- `commands.ts` — potentially surface auto-refs in issue display

issues.title: Live-updating web dashboard with `/project-serve` command
issues.description:
Add a `/project-serve` command that starts a local web server serving a read-only, live-updating dashboard of the project's epics, issues, and assets. Uses [json-render](https://github.com/vercel-labs/json-render) with shadcn components for rendering the project database. Every element has a "copy reference" button so users can paste precise references back into the agent chat.

**Architecture:**
- **Server (extension-side):** Node.js `http` module — serves static HTML + SSE endpoint. Watches `database.json` for changes and pushes updates.
- **Client (browser):** Single HTML page loading React + `@json-render/shadcn` from CDN (esm.sh). Renders a spec generated from the database. SSE connection for live updates.
- **No npm dependencies** — everything client-side loads via CDN, server uses only Node.js built-ins.

**Multi-project support:**
- Auto-finds an available port (starting from 3100, incrementing)
- URL includes a project identifier derived from cwd, e.g. `http://localhost:3142/project/a1b2c3/`

**Copy-reference:**
- Each element (epic, issue, asset, todo, research note) has a hover "📋" button
- Copies a reference like `[epic:abc123] Title` for pasting into agent chat

**Files affected:**
- New `dashboard-server.ts` — HTTP server, file watcher, SSE, spec generation
- `commands.ts` — register `/project-serve` command
- `index.ts` — wire up the new command

research.content:
## json-render Research

### What it is
json-render is a "Generative UI" framework from Vercel Labs. You define a **catalog** of components with typed props (via Zod), then render a JSON **spec** (tree of `{type, props, children}` elements) through a **registry** of component implementations.

### Key package: `@json-render/shadcn`
- 36 pre-built shadcn/ui components: Card, Stack, Grid, Heading, Text, Badge, Table, Accordion, Alert, Progress, Separator, Button, Input, Select, etc.
- Perfect for a read-only dashboard — Table, Card, Badge, Stack, Heading, Text, Alert, Accordion, Progress cover all our needs.

### Architecture Challenge
json-render is a **React framework** — it needs React, ReactDOM, and a build step (or CDN). The pi extension runs as interpreted TypeScript inside the agent. We need to bridge these:

**Proposed approach:**
1. **Server side (extension):** Use Node.js `http` module to start a server. Serve static HTML + SSE endpoint for live updates. Watch `database.json` for changes.
2. **Client side (browser):** A single HTML file that loads React + json-render + shadcn from CDN (esm.sh or unpkg). Renders the project database as a json-render spec. Connects to SSE for live updates.

### Spec Generation
The extension will need a `specFromDatabase()` function that transforms `database.json` into a json-render spec:
- Epics → Card with status badges, todos as checklists, linked issues
- Issues → Table rows or Cards with type icons, status badges
- Assets → Grouped by category in Accordion sections
- Each element gets a `data-ref` attribute for copy-reference functionality

### Multi-project / Unique URLs
- Auto-find available port (try 3100+, increment)
- URL path includes project identifier (hash of cwd or project name)
- e.g. `http://localhost:3142/project/a1b2c3/`

### Dependencies concern
- No npm dependencies needed in the extension itself — everything loads via CDN on the client
- Server is just Node.js `http` + `fs.watch`
- The spec JSON is generated server-side from the database

## Available shadcn Components (relevant subset)

| Component | Use case in dashboard |
|-----------|----------------------|
| **Card** | Epic cards, issue cards, asset cards |
| **Stack** | Layout — vertical/horizontal grouping |
| **Grid** | Multi-column layouts (e.g. epic grid) |
| **Heading** | Section headers (h1-h3) |
| **Text** | Descriptions, body text, muted captions |
| **Badge** | Status labels (draft/ready/in-progress/closed), issue types (bug/feature/chore) |
| **Table** | Issue lists, asset lists |
| **Accordion** | Collapsible sections (research notes, asset categories) |
| **Alert** | Warnings, info banners |
| **Progress** | Epic completion (todos done/total) |
| **Separator** | Visual dividers |
| **Tabs** | Switch between Epics / Issues / Assets views |

### Copy-Reference Design
Each rendered element wraps in a container with:
- A small "📋" button on hover
- Clicking copies a reference like `[epic:abc123] Title` or `[issue:def456] Title`
- User pastes this into agent chat for precise linking

autoValidation.strategy: User confirms the dashboard serves, live-updates, and copy-reference works in browser

issues.closeMessage: Dashboard server fully implemented with SSE live updates, json-render/shadcn UI, multi-project support, and top-level copy-reference buttons. Sub-item copy buttons tracked in ugf7af4l.

issues.title: Add copy-reference buttons to all atomic dashboard elements
issues.description:
Currently the dashboard's 📋 copy-reference button only appears on top-level Cards (epics/issues/assets). Sub-items like questions, research notes, todos, linked issues, success criteria, and asset sources have no copy button, making it impossible to reference them precisely in agent chat.

**Scope:** Extend `RefWrap` usage or add inline copy buttons to all atomic units:
- **Questions** → `[issue:id:q:0] question text`
- **Research notes** → `[issue:id:research:0] title` / `[epic:id:research:0]`
- **Todos** → `[epic:id:todo:0] todo text`
- **Linked issues** → `[issue:linkedId] title`
- **Success criteria** → `[epic:id:sc:0] criterion text`
- **Asset sources** → `[asset:id:source:0] path`

**Files affected:** `dashboard-server.ts` only — modify HTML rendering sections (~lines 213-260, 292-330) to wrap each sub-item with copy-reference functionality.

research.content:
## Current State Audit

**HAS copy buttons (via `RefWrap` on Card component, line 651):**
- ✅ Top-level Cards: epics, issues, assets

**MISSING copy buttons — all sub-items rendered as raw HTML or json-render Text elements without `RefWrap`:**
- ❌ Questions (lines ~213-230, HTML `question-card` divs)
- ❌ Research notes (lines ~234-244, HTML `<details>` elements)
- ❌ Todos (lines ~292-298, json-render Text elements)
- ❌ Linked issues (lines ~200-208, HTML `<a>` elements)
- ❌ Success criteria (lines ~302-305, json-render Text elements)
- ❌ Relevant files (lines ~308-312, json-render Text elements)
- ❌ Asset sources (not checked but likely same pattern)

**Implementation notes:**
- Issues use raw HTML rendering (`isHtml: true`), so copy buttons need inline HTML copy buttons (not `RefWrap` which is React)
- Epics use json-render spec elements, so they could use `RefWrap` — but sub-items are plain `Text` nodes without ref metadata
- The `copyRef()` JS function and `.copy-ref` CSS already exist and can be reused

issues.description:

**Rules:**
- Deferring an issue or epic requires **human approval** — an agent cannot defer autonomously
- Deferring an epic **automatically defers all linked open issues**

- `issue_reopen` / `epic_reopen` brings deferred items back

issues.title: issue_close silently fails to close when met: false — issue stays in-progress
issues.description:
When calling `issue_close` with `met: false` (e.g. skipping or abandoning an issue), the tool returns an error-style message (`❌ Requirement not met`) but **does not update the issue status to `closed`**. The issue remains stuck in `in-progress`, blocking other issues from starting and polluting `next_work`.

**Root cause:** The early-return guard at step 2 (`if (!params.met) return ...`) fires before `issue.status = "closed"` is ever reached. A `met: false` close is a legitimate workflow action (abandoned, skipped, out-of-scope) and should still transition the issue to `closed`.

**Fix:** When `met: false`, still close the issue (set `status = "closed"`, persist, clean up `closeReviewed`) but record `met: false` in the validation — consistent with how epic_close handles unmet criteria.

**File:** `issues.ts` ~line 313

issues.title: Unassigned bucket: future parking lot with smart context injection
issues.description:
Issues without an epic go into a virtual **"Unassigned"** bucket and are treated as **future/parked** — they never appear in `next_work` and are excluded from full session context. The project status header injects only a lightweight summary (e.g. *"3 unassigned ideas, 1 unassigned feature"*) so the agent is aware they exist without polluting context.

**Bugs are the exception** — unassigned bugs are always fully listed at session start since they are immediately relevant regardless of epic assignment.

To make an unassigned non-bug item workable, it must be explicitly assigned to an epic first.

`issue_list` gets a new optional `unassigned: boolean` filter parameter — when `true`, returns only issues with no `epic_id`. This is the explicit way to view and triage the parking lot.

issues.title: Config system with registry, defaults, and TUI widget
issues.description:
Adds a typed config registry to the project manager so runtime behaviour can be toggled without editing source. Config lives as its own object in `database.json` with hardcoded defaults that are always applied as fallback — so a "reset to defaults" is trivially possible. Solves the problem of hardcoded behaviour (e.g. workflow gates, context verbosity, future knobs) that currently requires a code change to adjust.

## Implementation

### `config.ts` (new)
Defines a `ConfigRegistry` — a typed map of keys to descriptors:
```ts
{ key, label, description, type: "bool" | "string" | "select", default, options? }
```
Exports `getConfig(r)` (merges DB values over defaults) and `setConfig(r, key, value)`.

### `types.ts`
Add `config: Record<string, unknown>` to `ProjectFile`. Bump `CURRENT_VERSION` for migration.

### `migrations.ts`
Migration to add empty `config: {}` to existing databases.

### `commands.ts`
`/config` command: renders all registry entries with current values in a TUI widget — booleans as toggleable checkboxes, selects as a cycling picker, strings as an inline edit prompt. Selecting an item updates it immediately via `setConfig`.

### `tool-docs.ts`
Document the config keys so the agent knows what's available.

## Initial config keys
| Key | Type | Default | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| `workflow.write_gate` | bool | `true` | Enable/disable the file-write gate |
| `context.unassigned_bugs_in_steering` | bool | `true` | Show unassigned bugs in per-turn steering |
| `context.brief_verbosity` | select | `"normal"` | `"minimal"` / `"normal"` / `"verbose"` |

issues.title: Git workflow integration with config-gated guards
issues.description:
Integrates git into the issue/epic lifecycle so the project manager can enforce branch discipline and commit hygiene automatically. Each guard is individually toggleable via the config system (depends on **[7hq83jxg]**). When `git.require_commit_on_close` is enabled, a commit SHA **must** be attached to the close message — `issue_close` is hard-blocked without it, and the SHA is stored on the issue as a permanent audit trail.

## Config keys (all under `git.*`)
| Key | Type | Default | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| `git.enabled` | bool | `false` | Master switch — all guards no-op if false |
| `git.epic_branch` | bool | `true` | Create branch when epic goes in-progress |
| `git.require_clean_worktree` | bool | `true` | Block issue start if worktree is dirty |
| `git.require_epic_branch` | bool | `true` | Block issue start if epic branch doesn't exist |
| `git.require_commit_on_close` | bool | `true` | Block issue close if no new commits since issue started |
| `git.require_commit_id_on_close` | bool | `true` | Block `issue_close` unless a valid commit SHA is provided |
| `git.merge_check_on_epic_close` | bool | `true` | Warn if epic branch not merged when closing epic |

## New files
**`git.ts`** — thin shell wrapper: `isClean()`, `currentBranch()`, `branchExists(name)`, `createBranch(name)`, `commitExists(sha)`, `hasCommitsSince(sha)`.

## Changes to existing files

**`types.ts`** — add `gitBranch?: string` to `Epic`, `startCommit?: string` and `closeCommit?: string` to `Issue`.

**`migrations.ts`** — migration for new optional fields.

**`issue_advance`** → in-progress: check clean worktree + epic branch exists (if guards enabled).

**`issue_close`** — add `commit_id?: string` param. When `git.enabled` + `git.require_commit_id_on_close`: hard-block if missing or SHA invalid (`git cat-file -t <sha>`). Store as `closeCommit` on issue. Show in output and `issue_show`.

**`epic_advance`** → in-progress: auto-create branch `epic/{id}-{slug}` (if `git.epic_branch`).

**`epic_close`** — warn if epic branch not merged into default branch (if `git.merge_check_on_epic_close`).

**`tool-docs.ts`** — document git params and config keys.

## Branch naming
`epic/{epic-id}-{slugified-title}` — issues commit on the epic branch directly.

## Dependency
Implement **after** [7hq83jxg] (config system) — all guards route through it.

issues.title: Auto-capture edited files as issue references with optional close-time change notes
issues.description:
When working on an in-progress issue, there's no automatic record of which files were actually touched — that context lives only in git history. This feature adds two opt-in config switches: one that silently captures every file written/edited during an issue and records it as a reference on that issue, and a second that gates issue closure behind a one-sentence "what changed" note per captured file. Both are disabled by default.

## Config entries (both default `false`)
- `issues.capture_edited_files` — auto-add written/edited files as issue references when an issue is in-progress
- `issues.require_file_change_notes` — block issue close until each captured file has a one-sentence change note

## Implementation

**`config.ts`**
- Add two new bool `ConfigEntry` items under the `issues` prefix

**`index.ts`**
- Hook into pi's write/edit tool events; when `issues.capture_edited_files` is enabled and an issue is in-progress, call `issue_research` with `type: "reference"` recording the file path (deduplicate — skip if already captured)

**`issues.ts` / close gate**
- When `issues.require_file_change_notes` is enabled, scan auto-captured file references on the issue at close time; if any lack a change note, block closure with a clear error listing the missing files

**Data model**
- No schema changes needed — auto-captured files use the existing research notes structure with a distinguishable prefix (e.g. `[auto] path/to/file`) so the gate logic can identify them

issues.title: Onboarding Mode — bypass all validation & steering for brownfield import
issues.description:
When onboarding existing/brownfield projects, users need to bulk-create epics, issues, set past dates, close items with backdated history, and skip all validation gates. A single config toggle `onboarding.enabled` would disable all process enforcement project-wide.

## Core Principle: Skip the Process, Not the Data Quality

In onboarding mode, issues/epics can be **created directly as closed** (skipping draft→researched→ready→in-progress progression), BUT:

- **Data consistency is still enforced** — you cannot close an issue without valid close data (evidence, message, met flag). The same fields required for a normal close are still required.
- **Missing steps are reported, not blocked** — if research is missing, if there are no todos, etc., the system returns an informational message to the agent noting what was skipped (the same things `advance` would normally enforce), but does NOT block the operation.
- This means onboarding can never produce "broken" records — every closed issue has proper close metadata — but the journey to get there is compressed.

## Onboarding-Mode Agent Steering

When onboarding mode is active, the extension must **inject its own steering context** that tells the agent:
- What onboarding mode means and what patterns to follow (e.g. bulk import, accept backdated data, skip validation)
- What workflow to use for efficient brownfield import (e.g. create epic → batch-create issues → close them with dates)
- That skipped steps will be noted but not blocked

## Race Condition: Conflicting Context Injection

The following context is injected on **every turn** via `before_agent_start` and must be **suppressed or replaced** in onboarding mode to avoid contradictions:

1. **Workflow policy** — "You MUST NOT write files unless an issue is in-progress" → contradicts onboarding freedom
2. **"No issue in-progress" warning** — steering to advance an issue first → irrelevant during bulk import
3. **Epic steering** (focus/next issue hints) → may conflict with bulk import flow
4. **Unassigned issues nudge** → noise during onboarding when everything starts unassigned

Additionally, the **write gate** (`tool_result` hook) blocks file writes when no issue is in-progress — must also be disabled.

Asset trigger events (on create/close/advance) may inject policies that conflict with onboarding patterns — these should be evaluated and conditionally suppressed.

## Key Changes
1. **`config.ts`** — Add `onboarding.enabled` bool config entry (default: `false`)
2. **`index.ts`** — When onboarding mode is on: replace all per-turn steering with onboarding-specific instructions, disable write gate, suppress conflicting asset triggers
3. **`issues.ts` / `epics.ts`** — Allow `issue_add` / `epic_add` with a `status: "closed"` param (onboarding only). Validate close metadata is present. Return informational messages listing skipped steps (no research, no todos, etc.) but don't block.
4. **`types.ts`** — Add optional date override fields (`closedAt`, `startedAt`) to issue/epic schemas
5. **Tool schemas** — Add optional `closed_at`/`started_at`/`status` params (only accepted in onboarding mode)
6. **Persistent onboarding banner** — Always-visible widget (like DEVMODE) so it's clear guards are disabled

## What It Does NOT Bypass
- **Close validation data** — evidence, message, met flag still required
- **Structural invariants** — "only one issue in-progress at a time" still enforced

issues.title: /idea, /feature, /bug commands don't display user input in chat history
issues.description:
When using `/idea`, `/feature`, or `/bug`, the user's prompt text is sent via `sendMessage` with `display: false`, so nothing appears in the chat history. The user loses their own input from the visible conversation. The fix: echo a visible summary message in the chat (e.g. `"New feature proposed:\n> <user text>"`) before/alongside the hidden steering message. Use appropriate emoji/label per command type (💡 idea proposed / ✨ feature proposed / 🐛 bug reported).

**File:** `commands.ts` — the `display: false` in each `sendMessage` call (lines ~155, ~188, ~217)

research.content:
## Fix Plan

Add a visible echo `sendMessage({ display: true })` before each hidden steering message in `commands.ts` for `/idea`, `/feature`, `/bug`, and `/spike`:

- 💡 `/idea` → `"💡 **Idea proposed:**
> {text}

*Reviewing...*"`
- ✨ `/feature` → `"✨ **Feature proposed:**
> {text}

*Analyzing...*"`
- 🐛 `/bug` → `"🐛 **Bug reported:**
> {text}

*Triaging...*"`
- 🔬 `/spike` → `"🔬 **Spike proposed:**
> {text}

*Reviewing...*"` (if it exists)

Each gets `{ triggerTurn: false }` so it doesn't trigger an extra agent turn.

issues.title: Centralized Context Engine for all agent steering and injection
issues.description:
All context injection is currently scattered across `index.ts` — session start, `before_agent_start` steering, write gates, asset triggers, epic focus — each with its own conditions and no unified view. A centralized Context Engine would register all context "rules" declaratively (with conditions, priority, mode filters like `onboarding`), compose them per-turn, and expose debugging/export tools so you can see exactly what the agent receives and why.

## Implementation Approach

### 1. Context Rules (steering/injection)
**`src/context-engine.ts`** — Core concepts:
- **Context Rule**: `{ id, label, condition: (state) => bool, content: (state) => string, priority, modes?: string[] }`
- **Registry**: All rules registered at init — workflow policy, write gate, epic steering, unassigned nudge, asset triggers, onboarding instructions, etc.
- **Composer**: Each turn, evaluates all rules against current state (config, mode, issues, epics), filters by condition, sorts by priority, returns composed context
- **Mode system**: `"normal" | "onboarding" | ...` — rules declare which modes they apply to (or exclude from)

### 2. Message Templates (presentation layer)
Currently every tool crafts its own formatting inline — mixing presentation with logic. Three output channels are used ad-hoc:
- Tool return (`{ content: [{ type: "text", text }] }`) — user + agent
- `sendMessage` with `display: true` — user-visible panel
- `sendMessage` with `display: false` — agent-only context

**`src/message-templates.ts`** — Centralized presentation:
- **Template Registry**: Named templates for every message type (issue_created, epic_advanced, close_checklist, etc.)
- **Channel abstraction**: Each template declares its channel (`tool_result | user_display | agent_context`) — the caller says *what happened*, the template handles *how it looks*
- **Separation of concerns**: Tool handlers return structured data (e.g. `{ event: "issue_created", data: { id, title, type } }`), the template system renders it for the appropriate channel
- **Consistent formatting**: All emoji, status icons, layout patterns defined once — change a template, change it everywhere

### 3. Debug/introspection tools
- `context_debug` — Shows which rules fired this turn, what content each produced, which were suppressed and why
- `context_export` — Dumps all registered rules with their conditions and mode filters as a readable map

### 4. Config integration
Individual rules can be toggled via config (`context.rules.<id>.enabled`), and mode switches like `onboarding.enabled` automatically filter the rule set

**What this solves for onboarding mode:** Instead of ad-hoc `if (onboarding)` checks scattered everywhere, onboarding rules declare `modes: ["onboarding"]` and normal rules declare `modes: ["normal"]` — no race conditions possible by design.

**What this solves for presentation:** Tool code focuses purely on logic and data — no more formatting strings inline. Adding a new tool output means adding a template entry, not crafting markdown in the handler.

research.content:
## Codebase Audit: All Context Injection Points

### `index.ts` — 7 injection points:

1. **Write Gate** (lines 82-113, `tool_result` hook) — Warns on file writes when no issue in-progress. Injects text into tool result content.
2. **Auto-capture edited files** (lines 116-137, `tool_result` hook) — Adds reference notes to in-progress issue. Not context injection per se, but a tool_result side-effect.
3. **Policy triggers** (lines 140-157, `tool_result` hook) — Fires asset-triggered policies via `sendMessage(display: false)`.
4. **Session dashboard** (lines 160-184, `session_start`) — `sendMessage(display: true)` with project brief.
5. **Session assets context** (lines 187-193, `session_start`) — `sendMessage(display: false)` with asset hints.
6. **Per-turn steering** (lines 197-288, `before_agent_start`) — The big one. Composes ~8 conditional sections into a single `sendMessage(display: false)`:
   - Current issue status line
   - Workflow policy (always)
   - Epic focus steering (in-progress epic only)
   - Issue focus with validation hints
   - Unassigned bugs
   - Unassigned other issues count
   - Dashboard URL
7. **Default renderResult wrapper** (lines 292-305) — Presentation layer for tool outputs.

### `commands.ts` — 4 injection points:
- `/idea`, `/feature`, `/bug`, `/spike` slash commands — each calls `sendMessage(display: false)` with agent instructions after creating an issue.

### Other files:
- `epics.ts`, `issues.ts`, `assets.ts`, `next-work.ts` — Return structured tool results only, no sendMessage calls. Clean already.

### Summary:
- **11 total injection points** (7 in index.ts, 4 in commands.ts)
- **3 channels used**: tool_result augmentation, sendMessage(display:true), sendMessage(display:false)
- **Per-turn steering is the largest** (~90 lines, 8 conditional sections)
- Tool files are already clean — they return data, index.ts does the steering

## Proposed Implementation Plan

### Phase 1: Core Engine (`context-engine.ts`) — ~150 lines
Create the rule registry and composer:

```ts
interface ContextRule {
  id: string;
  label: string;
  channel: "tool_result" | "agent_context" | "user_display";
  condition: (state: ContextState) => boolean;
  content: (state: ContextState) => string;
  priority: number;  // lower = first
  modes?: string[];  // empty = all modes
}

interface ContextState {
  store: Store;
  event?: string;        // which hook triggered
  toolName?: string;     // for tool_result rules
  toolInput?: any;
  inProgressIssue?: Issue;
  focusEpic?: Epic;
}
```

- `registerRule(rule)` — adds to registry
- `compose(state, channel)` — evaluates conditions, filters by mode, sorts by priority, returns joined string
- `listRules()` — for debug introspection

### Phase 2: Migrate Existing Rules — one rule per injection point

| Rule ID | Channel | Current Location | Priority |
|---------|---------|-----------------|----------|
| `write-gate` | tool_result | index.ts:82-113 | 10 |
| `policy-trigger` | agent_context | index.ts:140-157 | 20 |
| `session-dashboard` | user_display | index.ts:170-184 | 10 |
| `session-assets` | agent_context | index.ts:187-193 | 20 |
| `status-line` | agent_context | index.ts:202-211 | 10 |
| `workflow-policy` | agent_context | index.ts:213-214 | 15 |
| `epic-steering` | agent_context | index.ts:216-260 | 20 |
| `unassigned-bugs` | agent_context | index.ts:262-266 | 30 |
| `unassigned-other` | agent_context | index.ts:269-274 | 35 |
| `dashboard-url` | agent_context | index.ts:276-279 | 40 |
| `cmd-idea/feature/bug/spike` | agent_context | commands.ts:134-226 | 10 |

### Phase 3: Config Integration
- Each rule toggleable via `context.rules.<id>.enabled` (default true)
- Mode system: `context.mode` config entry (default "normal")

### Phase 4: Debug Tools (→ separate issue mz03veip)
- `context_debug` shows last compose output with rule-by-rule breakdown
- Gated behind `PI_DEBUG` env var

### What NOT to do yet:
- **Message Templates** — the issue description mentions this but it's a separate concern (presentation vs. injection). The tool files already return clean data. Templates can be a follow-up epic if needed. Keep scope tight.

### Migration strategy:
- Build engine + register all rules in one PR
- Each rule's content function is literally copy-paste of current inline code into a function
- Delete the inline code from index.ts, replace with `compose()` calls in the 3 hooks
- index.ts hooks become ~10 lines each: build state → compose → send

## Deferred Validation: Debug Mode Required

Full validation of the Context Engine should happen via the Debug Mode tool (issue `mz03veip`). The debug tool should support:

- **Transcript logging mode** — when enabled, captures ALL context steering (every `compose()` call), user messages, and agent responses into a queryable log
- **Tools exposed when debug is active:**
  - `debug_reset` — clear the log
  - `debug_query` — search/filter the log (by rule ID, channel, turn number, etc.)
  - `debug_dump` — export full transcript
  - `debug_rules` — show which rules fired/skipped per turn with evidence
- **Use case:** When implementing new features, enable debug mode to see exactly what context the agent receives, verify rules fire correctly, and diagnose steering issues — all natively without leaving pi
- **Validation of Context Engine:** Once debug mode exists, re-run through all rule paths and verify via the debug log that each rule fires with correct content, priority ordering, and mode filtering

issues.closeMessage: Context Engine implemented and verified. Core engine with rule registry + composer, 10 declarative rules migrated, index.ts reduced 27%. Mode system ready for onboarding. Debug introspection metadata built into compose() — full validation deferred to debug mode issue.

Refactored `index.ts` from 319→234 lines — all 3 hooks (tool_result, session_start, before_agent_start) now use compose(). issues.title: File attachments for issues, epics, and assets with Git LFS support
issues.description:
Issues, epics, and assets often need reference material (images, design files, PDFs, source files) but currently have no way to attach them. This feature adds an attachment system that copies files into a tracked `.pi/project/attachments/<entity-id>/` folder, automatically configures Git LFS for binary files, and links them to the entity's metadata. Attachments are reference-only — they inform work but aren't the deliverable.

## Implementation Approach

1. **`src/attachments.ts`** — New module handling:
   - Copy file into `.pi/project/attachments/<entity-type>/<entity-id>/`
   - Detect binary vs text (by extension or content sniffing)
   - Auto-add `.gitattributes` LFS tracking patterns for binary types (images, PDFs, etc.)
   - Store attachment metadata: `{ filename, originalPath, mimeType, size, addedAt, description? }`

2. **Schema/types changes** — Add `attachments: Attachment[]` to issue, epic, and asset types

3. **New tools:**
   - `issue_attach` / `epic_attach` / `asset_attach` — `{ id, path, description? }` — copies file, updates metadata
   - `issue_attachments` / `epic_attachments` / `asset_attachments` — List attachments (or a single generic `attachments_list` tool)

4. **Git LFS setup:**
   - On first binary attachment, check if `.gitattributes` has LFS patterns; add if missing
   - Track common binary extensions: `*.png, *.jpg, *.gif, *.webp, *.pdf, *.psd, *.ai, *.sketch, *.fig, *.zip`
   - Warn if `git lfs` isn't installed

5. **Existing `asset_source`** — Complementary: `asset_source` = "look at this external thing", `asset_attach` = "here's a copy tracked in the repo"

issues.title: Guard .pi/project/ folder from direct agent access via tool hooks
issues.description:
The agent can currently read/write `.pi/project/` files directly via bash, read, write, and edit tools — bypassing the project management tools entirely. This risks corrupted state, and any file paths in injected context could lead the agent to `cat` the database instead of using proper tools. This feature blocks direct access and scrubs references from all context injection.

## Implementation Approach

1. **Tool hooks in `index.ts`** — Intercept `bash`, `read`, `write`, `edit` tool calls. If the input path or command references `.pi/project/` (or configurable path), reject with an error message explaining to use project management tools instead. Patterns to catch:
   - `read` / `write` / `edit` with `path` containing `.pi/project/`
   - `bash` commands containing `cat`, `ls`, `grep`, `sed`, etc. targeting `.pi/project/`

2. **Context scrubbing** — Audit all context injection points (easier once Context Engine [ihkjviyl] exists) to ensure no raw `.pi/project/` paths are exposed to the agent. Replace internal path references with tool names (e.g. "use `issue_show`" instead of showing the DB path).

3. **Config** — `security.protect_project_folder` (default: `true`) — can be disabled if users want raw access

4. **Files affected:**
   - `index.ts` — Add tool hooks for interception
   - All formatting/context code — Audit for path leaks
   - `src/config.ts` — New config entry

issues.title: "Needs Review" flag to prevent premature work on rough ideas
issues.description:
When quickly capturing ideas/features, the description is often just a sketch — enough to not forget, but not enough to act on. Currently nothing stops the agent from picking these up as the next work item and building something half-baked. A `needs_review` flag marks issues as "captured but not yet thought through," blocking advancement to in-progress until the flag is removed after proper expansion.

## Implementation Approach

1. **`types.ts`** — Add optional `needsReview: boolean` field to issue schema

2. **Issue creation flow** — When creating via `/idea` or `/feature`, if the description is brief or the user indicates it's just a capture, the agent suggests flagging it with `needs_review`. Not default-on — opt-in per issue.

3. **`issue_advance`** — Block advancement to `in-progress` if `needsReview` is true. Return message: "This issue is flagged for review — expand the description and remove the flag with `issue_update` before starting work."

4. **`next_work`** — Exclude `needs_review` issues from being suggested as next work items

5. **`issue_update`** — Allow setting/removing the flag: `needsReview: true/false`

6. **Display** — Show a 🔍 or 📌 indicator in `issue_list` and `issue_show` for flagged issues

7. **Steering integration** — When the Context Engine ([ihkjviyl]) exists, add a rule that notes flagged issues separately: "X issue(s) need review before they can be worked on"

research.content:
**Key code locations:**
- `types.ts` Issue interface — add `needsReview?: boolean`
- `issues.ts` issue_advance — block in-progress when needsReview
- `next-work.ts` — filter out needsReview issues
- `format.ts` formatIssue — show 📌 indicator
- `issues.ts` issue_update — already supports arbitrary fields
- No migration needed — optional field

issues.title: Auto-notify agent when edited files are linked as sources in assets
issues.description:
When the agent writes or edits a file that's referenced as a `file` source on any asset, it currently gets no notification — so assets can silently go stale. This feature hooks into file-write tool results and cross-references the path against all asset sources, prompting the agent to review and update the linked asset if the change affects its content.

## Implementation Approach

1. **`index.ts` `tool_result` hook** — After `write`, `edit` (and optionally `bash` with file-write patterns), extract the file path and check it against all `asset.sources` where `type === "file"`. If matched, append a message: "⚠️ This file is linked as a source on asset [id] *title* — review if this change affects the asset content."

2. **Config** — `workflow.source_file_change_alerts` (default: `true`) — can be disabled

3. **Scope for future** — Could extend to epic/issue `relevant_files` too, not just asset sources — but start with assets since those are the curated knowledge base most likely to go stale.

4. **Files affected:**
   - `index.ts` — Extend existing `tool_result` hook
   - `config.ts` — New config entry

issues.title: Centralized Hook Registry for all tool lifecycle hooks
issues.description:
Tool hooks (`tool_result`, `before_tool_call`) are scattered inline across `index.ts` with ad-hoc conditions — write gate, file capture, source alerts, project folder guard, etc. A Hook Registry would let hooks be registered declaratively with conditions, priorities, and mode filters. This is the companion to the Context Engine [ihkjviyl] — together they centralize the two main extension touch points: what the agent *sees* (context) and what *happens* around tool calls (hooks).

## Implementation Approach

1. **`src/hook-registry.ts`** — New module with core concepts:
   - **Hook Rule**: `{ id, label, event: "tool_result"|"before_tool_call", toolMatch?: string|regex, condition: (state, toolInput) => bool, handler: (event) => result, priority, modes?: string[] }`
   - **Registry**: All hooks registered at init — write gate, file capture, source alerts, project folder guard
   - **Dispatcher**: On each tool event, filters matching hooks by event type + tool name + condition + mode, runs in priority order, merges results

2. **`index.ts`** — Refactor: replace all inline `tool_result`/`before_tool_call` handlers with a single dispatcher that delegates to the registry

3. **Shared infrastructure with Context Engine [ihkjviyl]** — Both registries share:
   - Mode system (`"normal" | "onboarding"`)
   - Config-based toggling (`hooks.rules.<id>.enabled`)
   - Debug/introspection tools
   - Extend `context_debug` → `system_debug` showing both context rules AND hook rules that fired

4. **Recommended build order:**
   - **Context Engine [ihkjviyl] first** — establishes mode system, config patterns, debug infrastructure
   - **Hook Registry second** — reuses mode system and debug patterns
   - **All other features after** (onboarding, source alerts, folder guard) — become simple rule registrations

5. **Files affected:**
   - New `src/hook-registry.ts`
   - `index.ts` — Major refactor of hook wiring
   - Shared types/infra with `src/context-engine.ts`

research.content:
## Post-Context-Engine Audit: Remaining Inline Hooks in index.ts

After the Context Engine refactor, `index.ts` tool_result handler (lines 72-142) still has 4 inline behaviors:

### 1. refreshStatus (line 73-75)
- **Trigger:** Any PROJECT_TOOLS tool call
- **Action:** Updates status bar + focus widget
- **Type:** Side-effect (UI update), no return value modification

### 2. Write Gate orchestration (lines 78-97)
- **Trigger:** Any tool call
- **Action:** Builds ContextState, calls `compose("tool_result", state)`, augments return content
- **Type:** Return value modifier — augments tool result with warning text
- **Note:** The *rule logic* is in context-rules.ts, but the *orchestration* (build state → compose → augment return) is still inline

### 3. Auto-capture edited files (lines 100-122)
- **Trigger:** edit/write tools with a path
- **Action:** Adds `[auto] filepath` reference to in-progress issue
- **Type:** Side-effect (store mutation), no return value modification

### 4. Policy trigger orchestration (lines 125-141)
- **Trigger:** Tools in TOOL_TO_EVENT map
- **Action:** Builds ContextState with policyEvent, calls compose, sends agent_context message
- **Type:** Side-effect (sendMessage), no return value modification
- **Note:** Like write-gate, the *rule logic* is in context-rules.ts but *orchestration* is inline

### Key insight:
Hooks have TWO distinct patterns:
- **Result modifiers** (write-gate) — return augmented content, only one can "win"
- **Side-effects** (refreshStatus, auto-capture, policy triggers) — run in sequence, don't modify return

The Hook Registry needs to handle both patterns.

issues.closeMessage: Hook Registry implemented. Core registry with two hook types (side-effect + result-modifier), 4 hooks migrated, index.ts tool_result handler reduced to a single dispatch() call. Combined with Context Engine, index.ts is now 173 lines (was 319) — all logic lives in declarative registries.

issues.title: Debug mode: show agent-only context and hide debug tools behind ENV gate
issues.description:
Add a two-layer debug system:

## Layer 1: ENV Gate (master switch)
- Debug features are **completely hidden** unless a specific env var is set (e.g. `PI_PM_DEBUG=1`)
- When ENV is not set: no debug tools registered, no debug config entries visible, no debug context injected — zero footprint
- This prevents the agent from even knowing debug capabilities exist in normal usage

## Layer 2: Config Settings (granular control, only when ENV is set)
When `PI_PM_DEBUG=1` is set, the following config options become available:
- `debug.show_agent_context` — Show agent-only messages (`display: false`) as visible messages so the user can see exactly what steering/context the agent receives
- `debug.show_hook_activity` — Show which hooks fired on each tool call
- `debug.show_context_rules` — Show which context rules composed the current turn's steering
- Future: `debug.log_to_file` etc.

## Implementation
1. **`index.ts` / init** — Check `process.env.PI_PM_DEBUG` at startup. If not set, skip all debug tool registration and debug config entries
2. **`config.ts`** — Debug config entries added conditionally to `CONFIG_REGISTRY` only when ENV is set
3. **Agent-only context display** — When `debug.show_agent_context` is enabled, intercept all `sendMessage({ display: false })` calls and flip to `display: true` (or duplicate as a visible debug panel)
4. **Debug tools** (registered only when ENV set) — `context_debug`, `hook_debug`, etc. from Context Engine [ihkjviyl] and Hook Registry [l8f1cbea]

Links: Context Engine [ihkjviyl], Hook Registry [l8f1cbea]

research.content:
## Expanded Scope: Full Transcript Debug Mode

Beyond just showing agent-only context, this tool should provide a full **debug logging mode**:

- When enabled, log every piece of context steering (all `compose()` results with fired/skipped metadata), user messages, and agent responses
- Provide native tools: `debug_reset`, `debug_query` (filter by rule/channel/turn), `debug_dump` (export transcript), `debug_rules` (per-turn rule breakdown)
- This enables the agent to self-diagnose steering issues when implementing new features — run debug mode, exercise a path, query the log, fix the rule
- Context Engine validation (issue `ihkjviyl`) is deferred until this tool exists

## Implementation Plan

### Phase 1: Debug Log Infrastructure (`debug-log.ts`)
In-memory circular buffer that captures every compose() and dispatch() call:

```ts
interface DebugEntry {
  turn: number;
  timestamp: string;
  source: "context-engine" | "hook-registry";
  channel?: string;    // for context: "agent_context", "user_display", "tool_result"
  event?: string;      // "before_agent_start", "session_start", "tool_result"
  fired: { id: string; label: string }[];
  skipped: { id: string; reason: string }[];
  output?: string;     // the composed text (truncated for large outputs)
}
```

- `logEntry(entry)` / `getLog(filter?)` / `clearLog()` / `getTurn()` / `nextTurn()`
- Max ~200 entries (circular), enough for a session

### Phase 2: Wire logging into existing systems
- `compose()` in context-engine.ts → after composing, call `logEntry()` if debug enabled
- `dispatch()` in hook-registry.ts → after dispatching, call `logEntry()` if debug enabled
- Increment turn counter in `before_agent_start`

### Phase 3: ENV Gate + Debug Tools
Check `process.env.PI_PM_DEBUG` at init. If set, register:

| Tool | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `debug_rules` | Show all registered context rules + hooks with IDs, labels, priorities |
| `debug_log` | Show recent debug log entries (filterable by source, rule ID, turn) |
| `debug_clear` | Clear the debug log |
| `debug_context` | Show what the last compose() produced for each channel |

### Phase 4: Config entries (conditional)
When ENV set, add to config registry:
- `debug.show_agent_context` — flip display:false → display:true
- `debug.verbose_log` — include full output text in log entries (vs truncated)

### What to skip for now:
- `debug_dump` to file — can add later
- `debug_query` with complex filters — `debug_log` with simple filters is enough
- Intercepting sendMessage for display flip — complex, defer to config entry

issues.title: Multi-project attach — manage multiple external project databases from one hub
issues.description:
Enable a "hub" repo to attach to project databases in other folders (e.g. `attach /path/to/repo-A`). The agent could then switch between attached projects — reading their epics/issues/assets, pushing updates, and steering work — all from a single pi session. This would let you manage 5+ repos' project state from a 6th central repo, with commands to list attached projects, switch active project context, and sync.

**Complexity:** High — the store, all tools, and all context steering currently assume a single `database.json` in CWD. This would require a project registry, path-aware store loading, and scoped tool execution.

**Value:** Very high — portfolio-level project management across multiple repos without switching sessions.

issues.title: File-per-entity data layer — replace single database.json with mergeable individual files
issues.description:
Replace the monolithic `database.json` with a file-per-entity storage layer: each epic, issue, and asset gets its own file (e.g. `.pi/project/issues/ihkjviyl.json`). This makes project data git-mergeable, enables file-level locking, and is a prerequisite for both multi-project attach ([eis1twji]) and agent delegation via worktrees. The store API (`load`/`save`) would be refactored to read/write individual files while maintaining the same in-memory `ProjectFile` interface for backward compatibility.

**Complexity:** High — touches every read/write path, needs migration from existing databases, index/cache layer for performance.
**Value:** Foundational — unblocks parallel agents, worktree workflows, and cross-repo project management.

issues.title: Agent delegation — spawn parallel agents on git worktrees for epic/issue work
issues.description:
Delegate an epic or issue to a separate agent working in a git worktree branch, enabling parallel development with PR-based merge back. The manager agent plans and delegates, worker agents execute in isolation, and results merge via PRs. Requires file locking on delegated entities to prevent conflicts. **Depends on:** file-per-entity data layer ([zp37hnsl]) — without mergeable individual files, worktree branches can't cleanly merge project state back.

**Complexity:** Very high — worktree orchestration, agent spawning, entity locking, merge coordination.
**Value:** Transformative — true parallel AI development with project-aware coordination.

issues.title: Soft delete assets — trash, undelete, and list deleted
issues.description: Currently deleting an asset is permanent. Soft delete moves assets to a "trash" state instead of removing them, allowing recovery. Adds `asset_delete` (soft), `asset_undelete`, and `asset_list` with `deleted_only` filter to browse trash.\n\n**Implementation:** Add `deletedAt?: string` to Asset type. `asset_delete` sets timestamp, `asset_undelete` clears it. All existing queries add `&& !a.deletedAt` guard. Files: `types.ts`, `assets.ts`, `format.ts`.

research.content:
**Implementation plan:**
1. `types.ts` — Add `deletedAt?: string` to Asset interface
2. `assets.ts` — Add `asset_delete` tool (sets deletedAt), `asset_undelete` tool (clears it)
3. `assets.ts` — Filter out deleted assets in `asset_list`, `asset_show` (show warning if viewing deleted), context injection, and tool-doc search
4. `assets.ts` — Add `include_deleted` param to `asset_list` to browse trash
5. `format.ts` — Show 🗑️ indicator on deleted assets
6. `tool-docs.ts` — Document new tools
7. No migration needed — `deletedAt` is optional, absent = not deleted

issues.closeMessage: Implemented soft delete for assets with trash/undelete/list-deleted functionality across all relevant surfaces.

Adds `asset_delete` (soft), `asset_undelete`, and `asset_list` with `deleted_only` filter to browse trash.\n\n**Implementation:** Add `deletedAt?: string` to Asset type. `asset_delete` sets timestamp, `asset_undelete` clears it. All existing queries add `&& !a.deletedAt` guard. Files: `types.ts`, `assets.ts`, `format.ts`.

issues.title: Issue-level implementation checklist (todos) with per-turn steering
issues.description:
Add a `todos` field to issues (like epics already have), with an `issue_todo` tool to add/toggle items. When an issue is in-progress, the per-turn steering should show uncompleted todos so the agent doesn't skip implementation items. For example: `→ IN PROGRESS: [mz03veip] Debug mode (2/4 todos: ☐ Add config entries, ☐ Add UX indicator)`. The agent should also be warned when closing an issue with uncompleted todos.

**Changes needed:**
- `types.ts` — Add `todos: { text: string; done: boolean }[]` to Issue interface
- `migrations.ts` — Add migration to initialize `todos: []` on existing issues
- `issues.ts` — Add `issue_todo` tool (add/toggle, mirrors `epic_todo`)
- `context-rules.ts` — Update `epic-steering` rule to include issue todos in the in-progress line
- `issues.ts` — Warn on close if uncompleted todos exist
- `tool-docs.ts` — Add docs for `issue_todo`

research.content:

issues.title: Dependency links with optional blocking semantics between issues and epics
issues.description: Add a `blocks`/`blocked-by` link type alongside the existing informational links. When an issue or epic is marked as blocked by another, advancing it to in-progress would warn (or block, depending on config) if the blocker isn't closed yet. **Config-controlled and default disabled** — `workflow.enforce_dependencies` (bool, default: false). When disabled, dependency links are purely informational. When enabled, `issue_advance` and `epic_advance` check for open blockers before allowing in-progress. The `issue_link` tool would get a `type` parameter: `"related"` (default, current behavior) or `"blocks"`.

research.content:
**Implementation plan:**
1. `types.ts` — Change `linkedIssueIds: string[]` to `linkedIssues: { id: string; type: "related" | "blocks" | "blocked-by" }[]` on Issue. Keep `linkedIssueIds` as compat alias or migrate.
2. `config.ts` — Add `workflow.enforce_dependencies` (default: false)
3. `issues.ts` `issue_link` — Add `type` param (default: "related"). When type="blocks", add "blocks" on source, "blocked-by" on target.
4. `issues.ts` `issue_advance` — When enforce_dependencies is on and advancing to in-progress, check for open "blocked-by" links.
5. `format.ts` — Show link type in verbose display
6. `migrations.ts` — Migrate `linkedIssueIds` → `linkedIssues` with type "related"
7. `tool-docs.ts` — Update issue_link docs

Actually, simpler approach: keep `linkedIssueIds` for backward compat, add a parallel `dependencies: { issueId: string; type: "blocks" | "blocked-by" }[]` field. This avoids breaking the existing link model.

issues.closeMessage: Implemented dependency links with optional blocking semantics. Config-controlled, default off, purely informational unless enabled.

When disabled, dependency links are purely informational. When enabled, `issue_advance` and `epic_advance` check for open blockers before allowing in-progress. The `issue_link` tool would get a `type` parameter: `"related"` (default, current behavior) or `"blocks"`.

issues.title: Issue attachment capture — store images, files, and references alongside issues
issues.description:
When creating issues via `/bug`, `/idea`, `/feature`, or `issue_add`, automatically capture any attachments (images, screenshots, file references, URLs) from the conversation context and store them as part of the issue record. This preserves evidence that would otherwise be lost when the session ends. Config-controlled (`issues.capture_attachments`, default: false).

**Changes needed:**
- `types.ts` — Add `attachments: { type: "image"|"file"|"url", path: string, description?: string, addedAt: string }[]` to Issue interface
- `migrations.ts` — Migration to add `attachments: []` to existing issues
- Store files in `.pi/project/attachments/<issue-id>/`
- `issues.ts` — Add `issue_attach` tool for manual attachment
- `hook-rules.ts` — Hook into issue creation to scan conversation for attachments
- `config.ts` — `issues.capture_attachments` (bool, default: false)

issues.title: /project-reset command — validate state and compact context safely
issues.description: A slash command that triggers the agent to validate project state before resetting context. Checks: no issue in-progress with unsaved work, no uncommitted todos, no open questions. Summarizes current state and confirms it's safe to compact. Prevents accidentally losing mid-work context.\n\n**Changes:** `commands.ts` — register `/project-reset`, visible echo + steering prompt that instructs agent to validate and then trigger compact.

research.content:
**Implementation plan**: Register `/project-reset` in `commands.ts`. The handler should:
1. Load store, check for in-progress issues, unanswered required questions, incomplete epic todos
2. Show a visible summary via `ctx.ui.notify()` listing any blockers
3. Send a hidden steering prompt instructing the agent to either warn about blockers or confirm it's safe to compact context
4. If safe, the steering should tell agent to use `/compact` (pi built-in)

Key references: existing `/continue` handler pattern (lines 61-147 in commands.ts), `load()` from store, issue/epic types.

issues.closeMessage: Implemented /project-reset command with state validation, visible feedback, and steering for safe compaction.

validations.evidence: Registered `/project-reset` command in commands.ts. It checks for in-progress issues and unanswered required questions, shows a visible notify with blockers or all-clear, then sends a steering prompt instructing the agent to either warn or proceed with `/compact`.

issues.title: /continue visible feedback — show current context before agent resumes
issues.description: When using `/continue`, nothing visible appears — the agent just gets a hidden steering prompt. Add a visible echo showing the current epic/issue context so the user knows what the agent is working from. E.g. `▶️ **Continuing...**\\n> Epic: [id] title\\n> Current: [id] title (status)\\n\\n*Reviewing next step...*`\n\n**Changes:** `commands.ts` — add visible echo sendMessage before hidden steering in `/continue` handler, pull current epic/issue from store for summary.

research.content: **commands.ts lines 61-147**: The `/continue` handler builds a rich status summary with active epic, issues by status, focus recommendation, and unplanned issues. It sends everything via `pi.sendMessage()` with `display: false` and `deliverAs: "steer"`. Fix: add a `ctx.ui.notify()` or visible `sendMessage` before the hidden one showing a brief summary of current context.

issues.closeMessage: Implemented visible feedback in /continue command. Users now see current epic, active issue/todo focus, and any direction hint before the agent resumes.

issues.title: Gated research mode — require verified sources before advancing issues
issues.description:
When enabled (`research.gated: true`, default: **off**), issues cannot advance past `researched` without linked research notes, asset references, or explicit justification. Enforces a "don't trust pre-trained content" policy — the agent must ground work in web searches, codebase exploration, or internal references. Bypass allowed with written justification recorded as a research comment.

**Changes:**
- `config.ts` — Add `research.gated` boolean config (default: `false`)
- `issues.ts` — Block `researched → ready` advancement when gated and no research/assets linked, unless justification provided
- `context-rules.ts` — Inject steering rule when gated: "Do not rely on pre-trained knowledge. Verify via web search, codebase, assets, or docs. Cite sources as research notes."
- `tool-docs.ts` — Update `issue_advance` docs to mention justification bypass

research.content:
**Key code locations:**
- `config.ts` CONFIG_REGISTRY — add `research.gated` bool (default: false)
- `issues.ts` issue_advance — add gate when advancing researched→ready: check issue.research.length > 0
- `issues.ts` issue_advance params — add optional `justification` string param for bypass
- `context-rules.ts` — add new rule when gated mode on
- `tool-docs.ts` issue_advance — mention justification

autoValidation.strategy: Enable gated mode in config, create a draft issue with no research, attempt to advance past researched — should block. Add justification — should allow bypass with recorded comment.

issues.closeMessage: Implemented gated research mode with config toggle, advancement gate, justification bypass, and per-turn steering.

validations.criterion:
When enabled (`research.gated: true`, default: **off**), issues cannot advance past `researched` without linked research notes, asset references, or explicit justification. Enforces a "don't trust pre-trained content" policy — the agent must ground work in web searches, codebase exploration, or internal references. Bypass allowed with written justification recorded as a research comment.

**Changes:**
- `config.ts` — Add `research.gated` boolean config (default: `false`)
- `issues.ts` — Block `researched → ready` advancement when gated and no research/assets linked, unless justification provided
- `context-rules.ts` — Inject steering rule when gated: "Do not rely on pre-trained knowledge. Verify via web search, codebase, assets, or docs. Cite sources as research notes."
- `tool-docs.ts` — Update `issue_advance` docs to mention justification bypass
validations.evidence: All 4 changes implemented: 1) research.gated config in CONFIG_REGISTRY (default: false), 2) issues.ts blocks researched→ready when no research and gated mode on, justification param records bypass as research comment, 3) context-rules.ts injects GATED RESEARCH steering, 4) tool-docs updated with gate docs. Runtime validation not possible but code review confirms all paths.

issues.title: /project-reset should be /reset-context and suggest /new + /continue instead of /compact
issues.description:

2. Steering tells agent to use `/compact` but should instruct: run `/new` then `/continue` in the new clean context

**Changes:** `commands.ts` — rename `project-reset` to `reset-context`, update steering prompt text.

issues.title: /project-clean-context needs a visible banner showing current context and reset readiness
issues.description:
When running `/project-clean-context`, the `ctx.ui.notify()` output is too subtle — there's no clear visual evidence on screen that the command ran. Need a persistent, prominent banner (like DEVMODE uses) that shows current context state: open epics/issues, blockers, what's next up, and reset readiness. The user should immediately see this without reading agent output.

**Changes:** `commands.ts` — replace or supplement `ctx.ui.notify()` with a visible `pi.sendMessage()` with `display: true` showing a formatted banner with context summary.

research.content: The `/continue` command uses a visible `sendMessage` with `display: true` — same pattern needed here. Replace `ctx.ui.notify()` with `pi.sendMessage({ content: bannerText, display: true }, { triggerTurn: false })` before the hidden steering message. The banner should show blockers/all-clear, stats, and next-up info prominently.

autoValidation.strategy: Verify commands.ts sends a display:true message with context summary in /project-clean-context handler

issues.description: The `customType` strings in `sendMessage` calls should be user-facing and action-based, not internal dev names. E.g. `context-reset-banner` → `context-reset-requested`. Audit all customType values in commands.ts and make them consistently action-based.\n\n**Changes:** `commands.ts` — rename all `customType` values to action-based naming.

assets.title: Reload before testing
assets.context: Before running any tests on pi extension functionality after code changes
assets.body:
After making code changes to the extension, always ask the user to reload pi before running any functional tests. The extension runs from TypeScript source directly and changes are not picked up until pi is reloaded.

**Rule:** "Please reload pi before I test this." — wait for confirmation before proceeding with tests.
